lines.text: [REDACTED]
On January 12, 1954, [REDACTED] East Orange,
NJ, appeared at the Newark Office and advised that he had received a letter
from one [REDACTED], West Austria,
on about December 20, 1953.
[REDACTED] stated that the letter requested him to attempt to sell
a number of inventions, which he, [REDACTED] had developed. [REDACTED] stated that
the request was to contact Aircraft Companies and Sewing Machine Companies
in the United States and attempt to interest them in these devices.
According to [REDACTED] requested that no governmental agencies
be contacted. [REDACTED] stated that the inventions, which [REDACTED] had developed
were new type of sewing machine and a flying saucer. [REDACTED] stated that he
came to this office because he felt that the flying saucer was of some
importance in the national defense of this country. [REDACTED] stated that he
has never met [REDACTED] but that [REDACTED] was given his name by [REDACTED] brother,
[REDACTED] who resides in Graz, Austria, and who is employed at [REDACTED]
[REDACTED], Graz, Austria. [REDACTED] stated that his brother worked with
[REDACTED] at one time, and he believes that [REDACTED] is a chemist or some type
of engineer. [REDACTED] stated that the contents of the letter seemed fantastic
to him and that if the letter were not written in such an intelligent manner,
he would have doubted [REDACTED] sanity. He stated that [REDACTED] claims to
have sold a new principle of aero-dynamics, which made it possible to
construct a practical flying saucer and that [REDACTED] had developed a model
that flies without the usual hazards. According to [REDACTED] the letter
continues by stating that [REDACTED] has been in contact with other men through-
out the world, who have been attempting to develop the saucer and that he
has been the first to win real success.
By way of background, [REDACTED] stated that he was born in the
vicinity of Leoben, Austria in 1925 and arrived in New York City on March
29, 1952 from Bremerhaven, Germany. He is employed at the [REDACTED]
[REDACTED] in Maplewood, NJ.
On January 30, 1954, [REDACTED] East Orange,
NJ, appeared at the Newark Office and advised that she was the wife of
[REDACTED]. She stated that another letter had been received from [REDACTED]
[REDACTED] by her husband, [REDACTED]. She stated that her husband did not
want to furnish the information in this letter to the FBI. She stated that
he was not aware that she was furnishing this information and wished to keep
the interview confidential.
COPIES DESTROYED
